metadata.title: Adaptive Self-Reflection as a Social Media Self-Effect: Insights from Computational Text Analyses of Self-Disclosures of Unreported Sexual Victimization in a Hashtag Campaign

abstract: Hashtag campaigns calling out sexual violence and rape myths offer a unique context for disclosing sexual victimization on social media. This study investigates the applicability of adaptive selfreflection as a potential self-effect from such public disclosures of unreported sexual victimization experiences by analyzing 92,583 tweets that invoked #WhyIDidntReport. A supervised machine learning classifier determined that 61.8% of the tweets were self-disclosures of sexual victimization. Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) analysis showed statistically significant differences in four psycholinguistic dimensions (greater use of past focus, cognitive processes, insight, and causation words) connected with reflective processing in tweets with self-disclosed sexual victimization compared to those without. Additionally, topic modeling and thematic analysis identified nine salient topics within the self-disclosing tweets, comprising three selfdistanced representations (i.e., relatively abstract and insightful construals) of the unwanted experiences: (a) acknowledging one's previously unacknowledged victimization, (b) reaffirming one's rationale for not reporting, and (c) decrying invalidating response to one's disclosure. Moving beyond reception effects and social support in extant research about social media as a coping tool, this study provides new empirical insights into the potential of social media to promote expressive meaning-making of upsetting and traumatic experiences in ways that support recovery and resilience.

paragraphs: Considerable research has examined social media as safe spaces for self-disclosing sensitive, distressing experiences such as intimate partner violence, sexual abuse, self-harm, and bullying that people tend to keep secret (Andalibi, 2019;Chu et al., 2021;Yeo, 2021). Much of this work focuses on more "private" disclosures within topic-based online communities (subreddits, Facebook groups, question-and-answer websites, story-sharing blogs, etc.) (Danielson & Emmers-Sommer, 2017;Yeo & Chu, 2017). With the rise of hashtag campaigns on social media in recent years, most notably the #MeToo movement to highlight the prevalence of unwanted sexual experiences through stories shared by survivors (Mendes et al., 2018), there is increasing research attention to online public disclosures of personal experiences around sexual victimization (Gorissen et al., 2023;Mendes et al., 2019). Previous studies have examined self-disclosures of sexual victimization by different populations using Twitter hashtags like #UsToo (men) and #ChurchToo (religious communities) (Bogen, Haikalis, et al., 2022;Bogen, Mulla, et al., 2022). Besides explicating contexts of sexual victimization, this line of research has highlighted the utility of hashtag campaigns for prompting online information seeking for sexual violence (Kaufman et al., 2021), and providing unique opportunities for publicly sharing personal trauma and connecting to others with similar experiences (Bogen et al., 2021).

While scholars have examined the supportive responses to disclosing distressing experiences of sexual violence in hashtag campaigns as a potential benefit for survivors (Bogen et al., 2019;Lowenstein-Barkai, 2021), they have largely neglected to consider how the act of writing and posting these experiences on social media may help the individuals themselves (Pingree, 2007;Shah, 2016, p. 20116;Shah et al., 2017;Valkenburg, 2017). To be sure, receiving social support online contributes to well-being and its availability is an important factor in disclosure decision-making (Chu & Jiang, 2024;Luo & Hancock, 2020). Nevertheless, prior research on online support groups has highlighted not only the larger beneficial effects from expressing support for others than being exposed to others' supportive messages, but also the enhancement of the reception effect from message exposure on well-being by the self-effect from message expression (Han et al., 2011). Self-effect refers to the impact that messages individuals create with the intention of communicating to others have on their own thoughts, feelings, attitudes, and actions (Valkenburg, 2017). Moreover, unlike online support groups, support-seeking is arguably a less salient communication goal for self-disclosure in a hashtag campaign than harnessing the tellability (noteworthiness) of one's firsthand account to raise awareness (Yeo, 2021), express solidarity with similar others, and challenge victimblaming attitudes (Mendes et al., 2019).

Drawing from extant theory and research on expressive writing and self-distancing, this study examines patterns of self-disclosures of sexual victimization in a hashtag campaign to explore the applicability of adaptive self-reflection as a potential self-effect from these disclosures. Using various computational approaches, we analyzed all publicly accessible tweets containing #WhyIDidntReport over a two-week period starting from September 21, 2018, the day on which then President Trump questioned Christine Ford's accusation against then Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh for sexual assault (Fortin, 2018).

Expressive Writing, Self-Distancing, and Self-Disclosures of Distressing Experiences Empirical research over several decades has repeatedly demonstrated physical and psychological health improvements from expressive writing about distressing experiences (Baikie & Wilhelm, 2005;Pennebaker, 1985Pennebaker, , 1997)). Initial explanations of these beneficial effects focused on emotional catharsis and disinhibition from confronting previously suppressed emotions (Pennebaker, 1985). Beyond mere venting of negative feelings, such confrontation entails translating the distressing incident into words, which should prompt cognitive integration and understanding of it, thereby relieving the physiological stress associated with inhibition and ruminations (Pennebaker, 1997). With mixed empirical support for these earlier proposed mechanisms, psychologists shifted attention to the therapeutic effects of narrative formation in structuring painful memories, leading to more adaptive schemas (Harber & Pennebaker, 1992;Smyth et al., 2012). The narrative approach in understanding psychological trauma and recovery highlights the role of survivors' stories in the recovery process (Tuval-Mashiach et al., 2004). Such storytelling opens possibilities for sexual abuse survivors to reconstrue their experiences in ways that offer closure and emotional relief (Harvey et al., 2000). A guiding assumption underlying these conceptualizations is that changing how people think about a negative incident is key to improving how they feel about it (Schartau et al., 2009).

Psychologists argue that the self-perspective individuals adopt when thinking about their negative experiences influences whether they engage in constructive reflective processing or succumb to rumination (dwelling on negative aspects) and feel worse (Kross et al., 2005). Converging evidence suggests that adopting a self-distanced perspective, rather than a selfimmersed one, facilitates adaptive self-reflection of the negative experience (Kross & Ayduk, 2011, 2017). Self-distancing involves stepping back from a negative experience and viewing it from a more objective and detached perspective, which allows for a broader understanding of the situation and the reconstrual of the experience in ways that support closure and emotional relief (Kross & Ayduk, 2011). Conversely, a self-immersed perspective involves revisiting negative experiences in the first person and focusing narrowly on concrete, especially emotionally arousing, features of those experiences, which can trap individuals in a cycle of reliving painful emotions and harmful rumination (Kross et al., 2005).

Several factors may affect the prevalence and potency of reflective processing in selfdisclosures on social media, particularly in the context of hashtag campaigns. Prior research suggests that platform vernaculars-conventions, affordances, and restrictions of sites-shape the nature and content of self-disclosures (Gorissen et al., 2023;Mendes et al., 2019;Yeo, 2021). Despite being highly visible and more "public," previous studies show that hashtag campaigns can trigger a flurry of self-disclosures of sexual victimization experiences (Gorissen et al., 2023;Mendes et al., 2018). We reason that these firsthand accounts by individuals who, at least in hindsight, recognized a past incident as an instance of sexual violence, entail a degree of distancing "the self-in-the-present-as-narrator" from "the-self-in-the-past-as-protagonist" (Apgar, 1998;Polanyi, 1982). Furthermore, we propose that hashtag campaigns driven by activist principles to challenge stigmatizing attitudes and prejudices should predispose people sharing personal experiences to focus on the broader context of sexual victimization and derive meaning from their experience by offering insights and counternarratives about their suffering (Mendes et al., 2018(Mendes et al., , 2019;;Yeo, 2021). In these ways, hashtag campaigns provide a unique context that encourages people to step back from their distressing experience and reflect upon it more effectively (Kross et al., 2005).

To investigate these conceptualizations, this study analyzed self-disclosures within the #WhyIDidntReport hashtag campaign. This hashtag emerged in direct response to Donald Trump's tweet questioning Christine Ford's sexual assault allegations against Brett Kavanaugh, who was then a nominee for Supreme Court Justice, prior to her testimony before the Senate Judiciary Committee. Trump's skepticism, suggesting that Ford should have reported the assault immediately if it was as severe as she alleged, led survivors of sexual violence to use #WhyI-DidntReport to illuminate the complexities of reporting and the potential outcomes. Unlike other notable campaigns like #MeToo, #WhyIDidntReport focused on explaining why victims opted not to report their assaults rather than detailing the assaults themselves (Griffin et al., 2022). Therefore, we anticipated that self-disclosures within the #WhyIDidntReport campaign will emphasize the expressive meaning-making of personal experiences of sexual violence, rather than solely recounting the specifics.

Our study investigated the extent to which the tweets that invoked #WhyIDidntReport indicate reflective processing of self-disclosed unreported sexual victimization experiences. We first built a machine learning classifier to detect the self-disclosing tweets, which not only provided the targeted data for analysis but also enabled us to gauge the prevalence of such tweets. To investigate whether and how the self-disclosing tweets illustrate reflective processing of victimization experiences, we analyzed their linguistic features and semantic structures. Certain patterns of language use reflect the building of meaningful narratives from self-distancing perspectives to explain distressing experiences (Park et al., 2016). We compared tweets with self-disclosures to those without for the use of cognitive processes, especially causation and insight, and past focus words, which are linked to improved analytic processing of emotional experiences (Pennebaker, 1997;Smyth et al., 2012) and temporal distance respectively (Park et al., 2016;Polanyi, 1982). To further explore how disclosers made sense of their experiences, we utilized topic modeling and thematic analysis to identify salient topics expressed in the self-disclosing tweets. Moving beyond discerning expressed reasons for not reporting (Griffin et al., 2022), we distinguished selfdistanced representations (relatively abstract and insightful construals) from self-immersed representations (recounting concrete features) among the machine-identified topics.

paragraphs: Using the Python package twarc, we searched and retrieved tweets written in English containing the hashtag #WhyIDidntReport via Twitter's Search API. To collect tweets over a two-week period, we ran the search on September 28, 2018, and October 5, 2018, excluding any previously collected tweets, as the API only includes tweets published within the past seven days. The dataset contained 92,583 tweets, spanning from September 21, 2018, to October 4, 2018. Each record comprised information including the tweet text, tweet unique number, time posted, and retweets count. The dataset was cleaned by removing usernames, IDs, URLs, symbols (e.g., "@" and "#"), and replacing netspeak (e.g., "bc" to "because").

paragraphs: Supervised Machine Classification. Supervised machine learning was applied to identify tweets with self-disclosure of sexual victimization. To carry out the supervised machine learning, the authors built a training set that comprised hand-labeled data. First, we randomly selected 600 tweets from the dataset using Python random module. Next, the second author manually labeled and classified the tweets into two categories: disclosure involved (n = 313, 52.17%), where posters reveal themselves as survivors of sexual violence by sharing their personal experiences of victimization and/or reasons for not reporting the incidents that happened to them (e.g., "I was nineteen. The guy grabbed my wrist and attempted to pull off my pants […] I managed to run away. I thought it did not count because I was not actually raped by him"), and non-disclosure (n = 287, 47.83%), where posters used the #WhyIDidntReport hashtag but without disclosing any experience of sexual victimization (e.g., "Dr Ford, you are a shining example of bravery and strength […] Thanks for speaking out. #WhyIDidntReport").

After building the training set, the data were preprocessed following the standard natural language processing practice (Hornik & Grün, 2011). Using Python packages NLTK and spaCy, we converted all the letters to lower cases, removed all the stopwords, lemmatized the words, and discarded punctuation, numbers, and white spaces. We proceeded to transform all the texts in both the training set and the final dataset into the Document-Term-Matrix (DTM) using the package scikit-learn. Subsequently, commonly used algorithms for textual classification, including logistic regression, k-nearest neighbors (k-NN), and Naïve Bayes, were trained with the labeled data to ascertain the most suitable classifier for this dataset. The k-fold cross-validation approach was used to validate the performance of the algorithms on the training data. We decided on the Naïve Bayes classifier, which achieved a mean accuracy of .91 and mean F1-score of .92, because it presented the best performance among the algorithms (see Table 1). The trained Naïve Bayes classifier was utilized to predict and classify the entire data.

Linguistic Analysis. To examine the psycholinguistic features of the #WhyIDidntReport tweets with self-disclosed sexual victimization compared to those without, we utilized the computer-assisted content analysis software Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) 2022 (Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010). LIWC has been effectively utilized to analyze psycholinguistic features in short texts. For instance, studies have used LIWC to compare linguistic profiles in suicide-related and non-suicide-related tweets (O'Dea et al., 2017) and to infer psychological distinctions between Democrats and Republicans based on their tweets (Sylwester & Purver, 2015). We complied the texts of all the collected tweets into an excel spreadsheet for analysis using LIWC 2022. Four dimensions of language use connected with reflective processing were examined: (a) past focus (e.g., was, had), (b) cognitive processes (e.g., but, if), (c) insight (e.g., know, think), and (d) causation (e.g., because, why). Independent t-tests were performed to compare the mean differences of these four dimensions between the tweets with and without self-disclosed sexual victimization.

While the LIWC analysis can reveal if self-disclosing tweets use more words related to reflective processing, it does not offer detailed insights into the patterns of expressive meaning-making that illustrate self-distancing and adaptive self-reflection. To delve deeper into this aspect, we used topic modeling to uncover the topics present in the self-disclosing tweets.

Topic Modeling. Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), a commonly used topic model that groups frequently co-occurred words in texts into coherent semantic themes (Blei et al., 2003), was applied on the 57,209 self-disclosing tweets. By providing the text input and setting the desired number of topics, the algorithm produces a set of topics, words assigned to the topics, and the topic proportions for each document (Zhao et al., 2018). Before conducting the LDA topic modeling, a dataset that included only the preprocessed text of self-disclosing tweets was created. We then tokenized the words and built the DTM. To determine the desired number of topics (k), we used the Mallet's LDA algorithm to perform eight topic models with k ranged from 8 to 50 (with an interval of 6). After evaluating the output of the eight models (top 10 words and tweets associated with each topic) for relevance and meaningfulness, we decided to use and report the results of the topic model with 14 topics as it had the highest topic coherence score (an interpretability metric), and the generated topics were comparatively meaningful than the other candidate models.

The output of the topic model with 14 topics-top 20 words and 50 associated tweets for each topic-were reviewed by both authors to evaluate whether each topic was coherent, interpretable, and relevant to self-distancing. To distinguish between self-distanced and selfimmersed topics, we excluded those focused on specific contextual details or emotionally arousing features (Kross et al., 2005). Salient topics related to self-distancing were further analyzed using reflexive thematic analysis to name and group them into broader categories.

sections.paper_id: Yeo and Chu - 2025 - Adaptive Self-Reflection as a Social Media Self-Effect Insights from Computational Text Analyses of
paragraphs: Reflexive thematic analysis was conducted for an in-depth understanding of the patterns of meaning within and across the machine-generated topics related to self-distancing. With the first two phases of familiarization and coding integrated into the topic modeling process, our thematic analysis encompassed the subsequent four phases of generating themes, reviewing themes, defining and naming themes, and writing up (Braun & Clarke, 2006). In the initial process of generating themes, the second author examined the top 20 words and 50 associated tweets for each topic to discern explicit patterns of meaning and assign appropriate labels to the topics. The nine topics were initially labeled using data-derived descriptions that captured the content of the tweets (e.g., "I've no idea what happened"; "I want to forget about it"; and "I need my job"). These topics were then grouped into denial, credibility, and security, reflecting the primary reasons why individuals did not report sexual assault. Upon reviewing these initial themes, we realized that solely addressing the rationale for not reporting assault would not reflect situations where survivors did report or tried to report the assault. Furthermore, while these themes were informative, they did not offer insights into the process of self-reflection.

Informed by previous research on social media distress narratives (Yeo, 2021) and digitized narratives of sexual violence (Mendes et al., 2019), we revisited our thematic analysis through a narrative lens, which allowed us to systematically examine how survivors make and remake the meaning of their experiences in their self-disclosures. The first author re-examined the top 50 associated tweets for each topic to identify the focal storyline (i.e., the subject or context of experiences shared), cross-checking these interpretations with tweets from other topics. Three separate but interrelated contexts were identified: the unrecognized, the concealed, and the discredited experiences, all highlighting the invisibility of sexual victimization. We then proceeded to elucidate the self-distanced representation within each context by examining how survivors positioned themselves vis-à-vis their past experiences, their interpretations and explanations of those events, and the introspective strategies that foster adaptive self-reflection (see Figure 1). To protect the anonymity of posters, the tweets cited in this article have been paraphrased.

sections.paper_id: Yeo and Chu - 2025 - Adaptive Self-Reflection as a Social Media Self-Effect Insights from Computational Text Analyses of
paragraphs: The machine learning algorithm classified 61.8% of the #WhyIDidntReport tweets with selfdisclosed sexual victimization (n = 57,209) and 38.2% without (n = 35,644). Independent sample t-tests showed that tweets with self-disclosures versus those without used more words connected with reflective processing-past focus (d = 2.02, 95% CI [2.03, 2.00]), cognitive processes (d = 0.69, 95% CI [0.70, 0.67]), insight (d = 0.23, 95% CI [0.25, 0.22]), and causation (d = 0.42, 95% CI [0.43, 0.41]) (see Table 2).

We verified that nine out of the 14 machine-generated topics entail self-distanced reflective processing of the negative experience. These nine topics constitute 66% (n = 37,566) of the selfdisclosing tweets. The remaining five topics were excluded from further analysis: one topic was not human interpretable, and four topics were identified as featuring self-immersed representations. Among these four topics (n = 15,887), three focus on contextual descriptions of experiences (e.g., "I remember the color of the blanket, I remember the design of the brass bed frame, and I remember what he said to me"), and one relates to emotional reactivity and difficulty in discussing the incident (e.g., "It happened more than 40 years ago and I still can't talk about it").

Through reflexive thematic analysis, we labeled and grouped the nine topics into three selfdistanced representations, namely, acknowledging one's previously unacknowledged victimization, reaffirming one's rationale for not reporting, and decrying invalidating response to one's disclosure (see Table 3).

sections.paper_id: Yeo and Chu - 2025 - Adaptive Self-Reflection as a Social Media Self-Effect Insights from Computational Text Analyses of
sections.header_norm: acknowledging one s previously unacknowledged victimization
paragraphs: The four topics categorized under acknowledging one's previously unacknowledged victimization center on survivors who did not initially identify their unwanted experience as sexual assault, or who chose to deny or suppress their memory of it. In these tweets, survivors acknowledged their naivety about the transgression, especially among those of a young age at that time (e.g., "I was 6 and he was 16. I thought there was nothing wrong cos I was too young to understand otherwise"), perceived complicity in what happened to them (e.g., "I thought I brought it on myself"), feelings of self-shame (e.g., "Because I was ashamed, and thought it would define me if it got out"), and avoidance of the memory (e.g., "Because all I wanted was to forget it ever happened"). This self-distanced representation involves a reflective process that engenders a realization of self-deception or misinterpretation of past events, prompting survivors to confront their sexual assault experiences alongside other survivors. This introspection is illustrated by a tweet: "Was grateful for having no personal story to contribute. Then realized I'd dismissed incidents that were kind of my fault, they didn't realize I meant no, I relented eventually, they didn't mean it that way. So yeah, there's a problem." The tweet showcases a self-distanced representation, with the poster alluding to detachment from past experiences to reevaluate them objectively with a renewed understanding. In these tweets, survivors acknowledge the personal barriers and internal struggles that hindered them from recognizing and reporting sexual assault, particularly feelings of shame and self-blame rooted in internalized victim-blaming attitudes (Bhuptani & Messman-Moore, 2019). One constructive aspect of this self-distanced representation involves reevaluating perceived culpability, which includes calling out the perpetrator's manipulative actions (e.g., "He used my virginity against me and convinced me that crying and pleading NO was normal, and maybe it was… for him") and rape myths that perpetuate self-blame (e.g., "Because I blamed myself. Because I didn't react the right way. But there is NO right way!!!").

sections.paper_id: Yeo and Chu - 2025 - Adaptive Self-Reflection as a Social Media Self-Effect Insights from Computational Text Analyses of
sections.header_raw: Reaffirming One's Rationale for Not Reporting
sections.header_norm: reaffirming one s rationale for not reporting
paragraphs: The three topics grouped under reaffirming one's rationale for not reporting-protect self and others, threatened by perpetrator, preserve career and livelihood-revolve around greater priorities in survivors' lives than reporting the assault and seeking justice. They illustrate survivors' rationality and sense of purpose in consciously choosing not to report the assault rather than emotional weakness or lack of understanding of the situation. These tweets affirm the desire to protect the survival and prospects of self and valued others as a valid, albeit undesirable, reason for not reporting (e.g., "He knew where I stayed and threatened to hurt my folks"; "Because he was my best friend and I didn't want to ruin his and his family's life"; "I was sexually harassed by my boss. I didn't report it because I needed the job to support myself").

This self-distanced representation acknowledges and validates past decisions where reporting was not pursued due to a practical assessment of circumstances. For instance, one tweet states, "I was 23 and just getting back on my feet," while another mentions, "My parents couldn't afford college tuition, and I needed that job," illustrating survivors' emphasis on personal and financial stability. This self-distanced representation encourages self-compassion by recognizing the decision not to report as a rational choice rather than a failed task, acknowledging the intricate personal and social constraints amidst power imbalances and fear of reprisals (e.g., "He was my boss. I would have lost my job and been blacklisted in my profession"). Furthermore, this selfreflection helps survivors develop a positive self-image by articulating their core values and priorities, such as the importance of kinship and the resolve to safeguard loved ones (e.g., "I had two daughters to protect"). It also empowers them to assert agency and reclaim control over their lives (e.g., "I wanted to move on from it, not have it consume more of my life than it already was").

Through reaffirming their past choices, survivors can see them not as concessions but as displays of strength and resilience in the face of adversity. Moreover, when motivated by advocacy, publicly sharing their sexual assault experiences can serve as a healing mechanism, enabling survivors to cultivate a resilient and activist identity while reshaping the discourse of sexual violence (Gueta et al., 2020).

sections.paper_id: Yeo and Chu - 2025 - Adaptive Self-Reflection as a Social Media Self-Effect Insights from Computational Text Analyses of
sections.header_raw: Decrying Invalidating Responses to One's Disclosure
sections.header_norm: decrying invalidating responses to one s disclosure
paragraphs: The two topics categorized under decrying invalidating responses to one's disclosure center on survivors' perceptions of not being taken seriously and the futility of reporting. In these tweets, survivors called out others' acts of ignoring, dismissing, denying, and victim-blaming, which engendered their reticence (e.g., "The first time I was sexually assaulted, people told me I was lucky he wanted me and accused me of cheating on my boyfriend. I was shamed and humiliated. The second time I was raped, I didn't tell anyone"). Survivors further decried the ineffectiveness of the justice system and the re-traumatizing process of reporting sexual assault (e.g., "Because I'll be humiliating myself while having to relive my nightmare to non-empathic cops and be further violated with a rape kit. The chances of him being convicted are zero so why go through with it?"). Underlying the invalidating responses is others' disbelief in their story, which emanates from a perceived lack of credibility, espousing common rape myths (e.g., "Nobody would trust me. I'll be labeled a liar and a cracked girl seeking attention").

This self-distanced representation confronts and resists the invalidating forces encountered when attempting to report sexual assault in solidarity with other survivors. By sharing personal accounts of unhelpful reactions and the struggles of reporting assault, survivors get to cathartically air their grievances and debunk misconceptions about the reporting process and outcome (e.g., "I reported to my campus health center, and they were like, 'Don't bother going to the cops, they won't do anything anyway.' They just told me to come back in a month for STI tests"). An essential element of self-distancing in this reflective process involves connecting with a collective identity by recognizing the struggles of reporting as a shared ordeal (e.g., "I did report and know firsthand how degrading and humiliating it can feel, which is why I stand with those who didn't report"). Through a hashtag campaign, survivors collectively transform their self-disclosures into a shared counternarrative (Mendes et al., 2019) that challenges dismissive attitudes toward the reporting of sexual assault, empowering them to reclaim their truth and shift from feelings of powerlessness to a sense of strength and agency.

sections.paper_id: Yeo and Chu - 2025 - Adaptive Self-Reflection as a Social Media Self-Effect Insights from Computational Text Analyses of
paragraphs: This study adds to the existing body of research on sharing distressing experiences, particularly sexual victimization, on social media by examining this phenomenon from a previously underexplored perspective. In contrast to previous studies emphasizing support exchange and reception effects, we explored the potential self-effect of adaptive self-reflection by identifying patterns of social media self-disclosures of sexual victimization that relate to self-distancing from the distressing experiences. Our findings are among the first to demonstrate the potential of social media hashtag campaigns to not only galvanize sexual victimization disclosures but also promote adaptive self-reflection of attendant distressing experiences within such disclosures. Machine classification detected self-disclosure of sexual victimization among most (61.8%) of the tweets that invoked #WhyIDidntReport in the large dataset, which illustrates the prevalence of sharing personal experiences in such hashtag campaigns (Gorissen et al., 2023). Our linguistic analysis pointed to psycholinguistic features of the self-disclosing tweets connected with reflective processing, which facilitates adaptive meaning-making of distressing experiences (Kross & Ayduk, 2011;Park et al., 2016). Furthermore, the machine-identified topics pertaining to self-distanced representations of personal experiences constitute most (66%) of the self-disclosing tweets.

These findings are noteworthy from a therapeutic perspective-particularly in identifying ways of changing how people cognitively represent upsetting and traumatic experiences to support recovery and resilience-and hold theoretical implications for understanding the beneficial effects of expressive writing in social media contexts. We identified three adaptive self-reflection strategies that survivors can use to navigate the challenges to their psychological well-being within contexts of invisibility regarding their sexual victimization. In the unrecognized context, sexual assault may be overlooked due to pervasive rape myths or internal suppression fueled by shame and self-blame. Here, the key strategy is to acknowledge the experiences as assault and resist self-blame by challenging internalized victim-blaming narratives and externalizing blame by attributing it to the perpetrator and harmful cultural norms. In the concealed context, survivors recognize the assault but choose to remain silent due to safety and well-being concerns. The adaptive strategy is to embrace this decision as a sensible and valid choice, acknowledging their resilience and strength in adverse circumstances to boost self-worth and self-compassion. In the discredited context where reporting of sexual assault is met with skepticism or further victimization, the strategy is to reclaim and amplify their voice by collectively confronting the invalidating forces encountered and speaking out against misconceptions of reporting, thereby regaining control of their narrative and enacting agency.

Another substantive contribution of this study is in illustrating the utility and robustness of various computational techniques for studying social media self-disclosures of sexual victimization, which can be readily extended to examine other forms of distressing experiences. Hitherto, only a handful of studies on online interpersonal violence discussions have utilized similar techniques such as machine learning to infer disclosure of sexual victimization in online messages and topic modeling to identify salient topics in support messages for intimate partner violence survivors (Chu et al., 2021;Gorissen et al., 2023). Most previous studies involve qualitative analysis and small data samples, which were neither representative nor generalizable. For example, a study based on 281 #UsToo tweets identified only six tweets as containing self-disclosure of victimization and concluded that Twitter users were unlikely to disclose personal victimization experiences using the hashtag (Bogen, et al., 2022aBogen, Haikalis, et al., 2022). By contrast, the results in this study based on a large and representative dataset support more definitive observations while the computational approaches performed well in detecting self-disclosing tweets and their psycholinguistic features and salient topics. We further demonstrate the complementary use of computational and qualitative methods to advance our research objective, exemplified by utilizing topic modeling to identify coherent, interpretable self-distancing topics for reflexive thematic analysis, providing fuller insights into the expressive meaning-making patterns in the tweets.

Notwithstanding these contributions, our results should be interpreted within the limitations of this study. First, the tweets analyzed were collected during the initial trending of the hashtag and were heavily influenced by the news events at that time, which may limit the generalizability of the findings. Additional research is needed to examine how patterns of expressive meaning-making vis-à-vis self-disclosed experiences vary with the specific characteristics of the hashtag campaign and social media platform. Moreover, our cross-sectional analyses of the self-disclosing tweets were unable to verify changes and outcomes, which calls for longitudinal and experimental designs in future research to test the processes identified from this study. Furthermore, this study is limited by the lack of information about the individuals who posted these tweets and their motivations for doing so. We did not attempt to capture the characteristics of posters (age, race, gender, etc.) that could provide richer insights owing to ethical concerns about informed consent and privacy. It is worth noting that tweets without explicit self-disclosure of sexual victimization in the hashtag campaign may have been posted by individuals who have experienced sexual violence but chose not to disclose their own experiences. Considering the self-related factors surrounding indirect disclosures (Andalibi et al., 2018), further research on social media selfeffects could compare the effects on survivors of sexual violence between disclosing and writing about their experiences in the hashtag campaign versus simply participating in the campaign by sharing content or expressing opinions. Additionally, our finding that a considerable proportion (28%) of self-disclosing tweets featured self-immersed representations, which hinder adaptive self-reflection, warrants further investigation into the tensions and potential negative self-effects of social media public self-disclosures to ensure safe and beneficial participation for sexual assault survivors.

Hashtag campaigns calling out sexual violence and rape myths offer a unique context for disclosing sexual victimization on social media. In this study, we explored the possible self-effect of such public disclosures of unreported sexual victimization experiences. Moving beyond the emphasis on reception effects and social support in extant research about social media as a coping tool, our findings offer new empirical insights into the potential of social media to promote expressive meaning-making of upsetting and traumatic experiences in ways that support recovery and resilience.

In the discredited context where reporting of sexual assault is met with skepticism or further victimization, the strategy is to reclaim and amplify their voice by collectively confronting the invalidating forces encountered and speaking out against misconceptions of reporting, thereby regaining control of their narrative and enacting agency.

Another substantive contribution of this study is in illustrating the utility and robustness of various computational techniques for studying social media self-disclosures of sexual victimization, which can be readily extended to examine other forms of distressing experiences. Hitherto, only a handful of studies on online interpersonal violence discussions have utilized similar techniques such as machine learning to infer disclosure of sexual victimization in online messages and topic modeling to identify salient topics in support messages for intimate partner violence survivors (Chu et al., 2021;Gorissen et al., 2023). Most previous studies involve qualitative analysis and small data samples, which were neither representative nor generalizable. For example, a study based on 281 #UsToo tweets identified only six tweets as containing self-disclosure of victimization and concluded that Twitter users were unlikely to disclose personal victimization experiences using the hashtag (Bogen, et al., 2022aBogen, Haikalis, et al., 2022). By contrast, the results in this study based on a large and representative dataset support more definitive observations while the computational approaches performed well in detecting self-disclosing tweets and their psycholinguistic features and salient topics. We further demonstrate the complementary use of computational and qualitative methods to advance our research objective, exemplified by utilizing topic modeling to identify coherent, interpretable self-distancing topics for reflexive thematic analysis, providing fuller insights into the expressive meaning-making patterns in the tweets.

Notwithstanding these contributions, our results should be interpreted within the limitations of this study. First, the tweets analyzed were collected during the initial trending of the hashtag and were heavily influenced by the news events at that time, which may limit the generalizability of the findings. Additional research is needed to examine how patterns of expressive meaning-making vis-à-vis self-disclosed experiences vary with the specific characteristics of the hashtag campaign and social media platform. Moreover, our cross-sectional analyses of the self-disclosing tweets were unable to verify changes and outcomes, which calls for longitudinal and experimental designs in future research to test the processes identified from this study. Furthermore, this study is limited by the lack of information about the individuals who posted these tweets and their motivations for doing so. We did not attempt to capture the characteristics of posters (age, race, gender, etc.) that could provide richer insights owing to ethical concerns about informed consent and privacy. It is worth noting that tweets without explicit self-disclosure of sexual victimization in the hashtag campaign may have been posted by individuals who have experienced sexual violence but chose not to disclose their own experiences. Considering the self-related factors surrounding indirect disclosures (Andalibi et al., 2018), further research on social media selfeffects could compare the effects on survivors of sexual violence between disclosing and writing about their experiences in the hashtag campaign versus simply participating in the campaign by sharing content or expressing opinions. Additionally, our finding that a considerable proportion (28%) of self-disclosing tweets featured self-immersed representations, which hinder adaptive self-reflection, warrants further investigation into the tensions and potential negative self-effects of social media public self-disclosures to ensure safe and beneficial participation for sexual assault survivors.

Hashtag campaigns calling out sexual violence and rape myths offer a unique context for disclosing sexual victimization on social media. In this study, we explored the possible self-effect of such public disclosures of unreported sexual victimization experiences. Moving beyond the emphasis on reception effects and social support in extant research about social media as a coping tool, our findings offer new empirical insights into the potential of social media to promote expressive meaning-making of upsetting and traumatic experiences in ways that support recovery and resilience.
sections.preview_head: This study adds to the existing body of research on sharing distressing experiences, particularly sexual victimization, on social media by examining this phenomenon from a previously underexplored perspective. In contrast to previous studies emphasizing support exchange and reception effects, we explored the potential self-effect of adaptive self-reflection by identifying patterns of social media self-disclosures of sexual victimization that relate to self-distancing from the distressing experiences. Our findings are among the first to demonstrate the potential of social media hashtag campaigns to not only galvanize sexual victimization disclosures but also promote adaptive self-reflection of attendant distressing experiences within such disclosures.
